key_points: A triangle has three sides, three angles, and important centers: centroid, incenter, circumcenter, orthocenter
Sum of interior angles = 180°; sum of exterior angles = 360°
Cevians: line segments joining a vertex to the opposite side (median, altitude, angle bisector, perpendicular bisector)
Median: joins vertex to midpoint of opposite side; centroid divides median in 2:1 ratio
Altitude: perpendicular from vertex to opposite side; orthocenter = intersection of altitudes
Angle bisector: divides opposite side in the ratio of adjacent sides; incenter = intersection of bisectors
Perpendicular bisector: intersects opposite side at 90°; circumcenter = intersection point, equidistant from vertices
Important theorems: Pythagoras theorem, Triangle inequality theorem, Basic proportionality theorem (Thales’ theorem)
Similarity and congruence criteria: SSS, SAS, ASA, AAS, RHS, AA for similarity
Special triangles: equilateral, isosceles, right-angled, scalene, with properties of sides, angles, medians, and altitudes
examples: Centroid: For ΔABC with medians AD, BE, CF, intersection G divides each median in 2:1
Incenter: intersection of angle bisectors of ΔABC; equidistant from all sides
Circumcenter: intersection of perpendicular bisectors; radius = distance to any vertex
Median formula: m_a = 1/2 √(2b² + 2c² − a²), e.g., for sides a=5, b=6, c=7, m_a = √(2*36+2*49−25)/2 ≈ 5.36
exam_tips: Always label vertices, sides, angles, and important points in diagrams
Use median, altitude, and bisector formulas for advanced problems
For Heron’s formula, calculate semi-perimeter first
Apply similarity and congruence theorems carefully
Remember centroid, circumcenter, and incenter properties for coordinate or length problems
Use Pythagoras theorem for right triangles or to find missing sides
